selected.addressed_gap: Existing work describes ACN behavior empirically; there's no theoretical understanding of why additive connections cause information to be pushed to early layers. This gap limits principled improvements and extensions.

selected.idea_title: Auto-Compressing Transformers with Layer-wise Additive Attention
selected.idea_description: Apply the ACN principle to Transformers by adding direct, additive connections from each transformer layer's output to the final classification token or sequence representation. This creates deep supervision in Transformers, potentially enabling auto-compression of deeper layers and improving training dynamics for very deep Transformers. The additive connections could be implemented via linear projections to the final head.
selected.key_innovation: Extends the ACN concept from convolutional to attention-based architectures, addressing the lack of work on how auto-compression manifests in Transformers where residual connections are also prevalent but have different dynamics.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors focused on CNNs (ResNets, DenseNets); no prior work explores how additive long-range connections to the output affect training and redundancy in deep Transformers, a gap given their dominance in modern AI.

selected.idea_title: Dynamic Exemplar Selection via Online Submodular Optimization for Evolving Graphs
selected.idea_description: This research would develop algorithms to maintain a set of global exemplars as the graph and GNN model update over time, using online submodular maximization with theoretical guarantees. The method would efficiently update coverage as new nodes/edges arrive without recomputing the entire RkNN structure from scratch.
selected.key_innovation: Applies online submodular optimization to maintain a dynamic set of representative exemplars that adapts to graph changes, unlike static one-time selection.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors assume a static graph, but real-world graphs evolve; this addresses the lack of incremental explanation maintenance in dynamic settings.

selected.idea_title: Certified Robustness for Exemplar-Based Global Explanations in GNNs
selected.idea_description: This work would provide formal certificates that the selected exemplars and their derived rules remain stable under small perturbations to the graph structure or node features. By integrating randomized smoothing with RkNN-based coverage, the method would quantify and ensure explanation reliability.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces formal robustness guarantees for exemplar-based explanations, ensuring they don’t change drastically under adversarial noise.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors do not address adversarial robustness of explanations; this provides theoretical and empirical guarantees against explanation instability.

selected.idea_title: Hierarchical Exemplar Discovery with Multi-Resolution Graph Coarsening
selected.idea_description: This idea proposes to first coarsen the graph into multiple hierarchical levels, then run exemplar selection at each level to produce a set of explanations at different granularities. Users could interactively explore from broad category-level exemplars to detailed subgraph-level patterns.
selected.key_innovation: Combines graph coarsening with exemplar selection to yield a multi-scale explanatory hierarchy, not just a single global summary.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors produce a single set of global exemplars; this provides explanations at multiple levels of abstraction for richer interpretability.

selected.idea_title: Cross-Modal Exemplar Attribution for Heterogeneous Information Networks
selected.idea_description: This research extends the RkNN-based exemplar selection to heterogeneous graphs with multiple node/edge types. It would design type-aware similarity metrics and coverage functions, then attribute predictions to exemplar nodes/edges across different modalities, generating modal-specific rules.
selected.key_innovation: Adapts the exemplar framework to heterogeneous graphs by handling multi-modal embeddings and generating explanations that respect type semantics.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors assume homogeneous graphs; this addresses the need for explainability in heterogeneous information networks where node/edge types matter.

selected.idea_title: Causal Exemplar Intervention to Isolate Decision-Relevant Graph Substructures
selected.idea_description: This idea combines causal inference with exemplar-based explanation by intervening on the graph structure around selected exemplars. By measuring how predictions change when removing or altering edges in an exemplar’s neighborhood, the method identifies which local structures causally drive class predictions.
selected.key_innovation: Uses causal interventions on exemplar neighborhoods to distinguish correlative from causally relevant graph structures.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors rely on correlation-based coverage; this adds a causal perspective to identify truly decision-relevant motifs and features.

selected.idea_title: Federated Exemplar Selection for Privacy-Preserving Global GNN Explanation
selected.idea_description: This work designs a federated learning framework where multiple parties collaboratively select global exemplars without sharing raw graph data. Using secure aggregation of gradient-based representations, the method would compute a consensus set of representative nodes across distributed graphs.
selected.key_innovation: Enables collaborative exemplar selection across decentralized graphs while preserving data privacy via federated optimization.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors assume centralized data access; this addresses privacy and collaboration constraints in real-world graph data distributions.

selected.idea_title: Exemplar-Based Explanation for Graph Self-Supervised Learning Paradigms
selected.idea_description: This research adapts the exemplar framework to explain self-supervised GNNs (e.g., contrastive learning) by selecting representative positive pairs or augmented views. It would generate rules explaining why certain nodes are pulled together or pushed apart in the embedding space.
selected.key_innovation: Extends exemplar-based explanation from classification to self-supervised learning, covering a broader range of modern graph learning methods.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors focus on supervised GNNs; this addresses the lack of explanation methods for self-supervised and unsupervised graph representation learning.

selected.idea_title: Interactive Exemplar Refinement with User-in-the-Loop Active Learning
selected.idea_description: This idea creates an interactive system where users can label or adjust proposed exemplars (e.g., mark a node as unrepresentative), and the algorithm actively queries the most informative nodes for the user to evaluate next, refining the global explanation over time.
selected.key_innovation: Integrates human feedback via active learning to iteratively improve exemplar selection and rule quality, closing the loop between explanation and user judgment.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors treat explanation as a one-shot process; this allows dynamic user interaction to correct and personalize the global summary.

selected.idea_title: Incremental Exemplar Selection for Evolving Graph Neural Networks
selected.idea_description: This idea proposes algorithms to update exemplar sets incrementally as graphs change over time, using dynamic RkNN computations to maintain coverage without full re-computation. It enables efficient explanations for temporal or streaming graphs common in real-world applications.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces online and incremental versions of RkNN for dynamic graphs, allowing exemplar-based explanations to adapt in real-time with minimal computational overhead.
selected.addressed_gap: GnnXemplar assumes static graphs; this addresses the limitation in handling evolving networks where graph structure changes frequently.

selected.idea_title: Cross-Domain Exemplar Transfer for Few-Shot Graph Learning
selected.idea_description: This approach transfers exemplar sets from domains with rich explanations to new domains with limited data. The system would identify transferable explanation patterns across graph domains and adapt them to new contexts.
selected.key_innovation: Applies transfer learning principles to explanations themselves, enabling explanation reuse across different graph domains rather than learning explanations from scratch each time.
selected.addressed_gap: Existing methods require sufficient data for reliable exemplar selection; this addresses the limitation in data-scarce scenarios where traditional approaches fail.

selected.idea_title: Top-k MaxSup: Extending the logit penalty to the k largest logits
selected.idea_description: This idea generalizes the MaxSup penalty to suppress the top-k logits rather than only the single largest. This could provide a stronger regularization signal by explicitly discouraging a small set of logit values from dominating the distribution, potentially offering finer control over the prediction sharpness and improving calibration.
selected.key_innovation: Extends the core MaxSup principle from a single logit to a set of logits, enabling a tunable regularization strength based on the number of dominant predictions.
selected.addressed_gap: MaxSup focuses solely on the maximum logit, which may be insufficient to prevent a small cluster of high-confidence predictions from forming, especially in multi-label or fine-grained classification scenarios.

selected.idea_title: Dynamic MaxSup with curriculum-based schedule for logit penalty strength
selected.idea_description: Propose a training regime where the MaxSup penalty strength is dynamically adjusted based on a curriculum, such as starting with a weaker penalty and increasing it as training progresses, or modulating it based on the model's current calibration error. This could optimize the trade-off between regularization and learning speed.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces a temporal dimension to MaxSup, adapting its regularization behavior throughout the training process rather than applying a static penalty.
selected.addressed_gap: The static nature of MaxSup (like LS) does not account for the evolving dynamics of network confidence and representational collapse during different phases of training.

selected.idea_title: Theoretical analysis of MaxSup's effect on the optimization landscape of neural networks
selected.idea_description: Conduct a formal analysis of how the MaxSup penalty modifies the loss function's Hessian and gradient dynamics. Compare this with the landscapes induced by cross-entropy and label smoothing to explain empirically observed benefits like reduced error amplification and preserved intra-class diversity.
selected.key_innovation: Provides a rigorous theoretical framework for MaxSup, moving beyond analytical decomposition of LS to explain its optimization-level advantages.
selected.addressed_gap: While MaxSup is motivated by analytical insights, its precise effects on the optimization dynamics and convergence properties remain an open question.

selected.idea_title: Self-supervised pre-training with MaxSup regularization for representation learning
selected.idea_description: Integrate a MaxSup-style penalty as a regularization term into self-supervised learning objectives (e.g., SimCLR, BYOL). The goal is to prevent the learned feature representations from becoming overly clustered or collapsed, potentially improving the quality and diversity of representations for downstream tasks.
selected.key_innovation: Applies the core idea of logit suppression—which operates on model outputs—to the internal feature space in a self-supervised context, targeting representational collapse without labeled data.
selected.addressed_gap: Supervised contrastive learning requires labels and uses a specialized loss. This idea explores using a simpler, logit-suppression-based regularizer for representation quality in a label-agnostic framework.

selected.idea_title: MaxSup for robust learning in low-data and imbalanced regimes
selected.idea_description: Investigate MaxSup's efficacy in scenarios with limited or imbalanced data. By preventing the model from becoming overconfident on a few seen examples, MaxSup could enhance generalization and reduce the amplification of biases present in skewed datasets, offering a simple regularization alternative to more complex re-sampling or re-weighting methods.
selected.key_innovation: Tests and adapts MaxSup specifically for challenging data regimes where overfitting and overconfidence are acute problems, a context not deeply explored by its predecessors.
selected.addressed_gap: Existing analysis of LS and MaxSup primarily uses large, balanced datasets like ImageNet. Their behavior under data scarcity or imbalance is a critical practical gap.

selected.idea_title: Unifying MaxSup and Neural Collapse theory to explicitly design against feature collapse
selected.idea_description: Formally connect the MaxSup objective to the Neural Collapse framework. Design a loss term or training protocol that directly penalizes the conditions for collapse (e.g., minimal intra-class variance, maximal inter-class separation) using insights from MaxSup's logit suppression mechanism.
selected.key_innovation: Moves from using Neural Collapse as an analysis lens to using it as a prescriptive design principle, creating a direct feedback loop between the theory of collapse and the regularizer design.
selected.addressed_gap: Papyan et al.'s work describes collapse under cross-entropy and LS, and MaxSup mitigates it empirically. There is no direct algorithmic guidance from collapse theory to build a better regularizer.

selected.idea_title: Calibration-aware training objective based on MaxSup with temperature scaling
selected.idea_description: Formulate a training loss that combines MaxSup with an explicit, differentiable calibration loss (e.g., based on Expected Calibration Error). This would jointly optimize for accuracy, low overconfidence (via MaxSup), and good calibration (via the direct calibration loss), potentially outperforming sequential methods (train then calibrate).
selected.key_innovation: Unifies the training-time regularization philosophy of MaxSup with the direct optimization of calibration metrics, addressing both overconfidence and calibration error simultaneously in the training loop.
selected.addressed_gap: Calibration is often addressed post-hoc (e.g., temperature scaling). While MaxSup improves calibration as a byproduct, this idea directly targets calibration during training as a primary objective.

selected.idea_title: Applying MaxSup-style logit penalty in multi-task and multi-modal learning
selected.idea_description: Explore using a MaxSup penalty on the logits of individual tasks in a multi-task model or on the outputs of different modalities in a multi-modal architecture. This could prevent any single task or modality from dominating the shared representation and encourage more balanced and diverse feature learning.
selected.key_innovation: Transports the concept of suppressing over-dominant outputs from a single-task classification setting to complex learning architectures with multiple heads or streams.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor work and MaxSup are studied in isolated classification tasks. The potential of controlled logit suppression to improve representation sharing and reduce negative transfer in complex setups is unexplored.

selected.idea_title: Adaptive MaxSup via Curriculum-Based Logit Suppression Scheduling
selected.idea_description: This idea proposes to implement a scheduling mechanism for the MaxSup penalty that evolves with the training process, similar to curriculum learning. By gradually increasing the penalty or changing its targets, it aims to optimize regularization and prevent issues like under- or over-regularization at different stages.
selected.key_innovation: The novel integration of curriculum learning with MaxSup, making the regularization adaptive to training progress, which is not present in static LS or MaxSup.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the limitation of uniform regularization throughout training, as current methods apply fixed penalties that may not be optimal for all training phases.

selected.idea_title: Leveraging Logit Suppression to Enhance Adversarial Robustness in Neural Networks
selected.idea_description: Use MaxSup's logit suppression to reduce overconfidence, which is often exploited in adversarial attacks. By making predictions less confident, the model could become more robust against perturbations.
selected.key_innovation: Directly links logit suppression to adversarial robustness, applying MaxSup as a defense mechanism by mitigating overconfidence vulnerabilities.
selected.addressed_gap: Adversarial robustness is not covered in the predecessors; overconfidence highlighted by Guo et al. could be a vulnerability, so this extends the research direction to security applications.

selected.idea_title: Real-Time Adaptive Question Generation for Scalable 3D Scene Retrieval
selected.idea_description: This idea proposes a method to generate and rank questions in real-time for large-scale 3D scene databases, using a lightweight model that balances information gain and computational cost. The system adapts question strategies based on the current retrieval state and user responses.
selected.key_innovation: Unlike previous works that assume fixed computational budgets, this method introduces a scalable question generation framework that can operate in real-time by pruning the search space of questions and using efficient encoders.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the scalability limitation in existing interactive retrieval systems, which may not be applicable to large databases due to high computational cost.

selected.idea_title: Reinforcement Learning for Question Selection with Immediate User Feedback
selected.idea_description: We propose training a question selection policy using reinforcement learning, where the reward is derived from immediate user feedback (e.g., the user's rating of the retrieved scenes after each question). This allows the system to learn from actual user interactions and improve over time.
selected.key_innovation: Instead of using a fixed information-theoretic objective (like EVPI), the system learns to select questions that maximize long-term user satisfaction through trial and error.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors rely on predefined objectives (like EVPI) which may not capture user preferences or the dynamic nature of conversation.

selected.idea_title: Multi-Modal Interactive Retrieval with Sketches and Text for 3D Scenes
selected.idea_description: This system allows users to provide not only textual queries and answers but also sketches or partial 3D models. The questioner can then generate questions that reference both modalities to disambiguate the scene.
selected.key_innovation: Integrates non-textual modalities (sketches) into the interactive refinement loop, which can convey spatial and shape information that text cannot.
selected.addressed_gap: Current methods are limited to text-only interaction, which may be insufficient for complex 3D scenes.

selected.idea_title: Hybrid Question Generation: Combining Templates and Neural Models for Effective Disambiguation
selected.idea_description: We develop a hybrid question generation system that uses a neural model to generate novel questions and a template-based system for high-confidence, structured questions. The system dynamically chooses between the two based on the current context.
selected.key_innovation: Combines the reliability and coverage of templates with the flexibility and novelty of neural generation, leading to more effective and diverse questions.
selected.addressed_gap: Existing methods either use templates (which are limited) or neural generation (which can be unreliable), and this hybrid approach mitigates the weaknesses of both.

selected.idea_title: Benchmark and Evaluation Protocol for Interactive 3D Scene Retrieval with Simulated Users
selected.idea_description: We create a benchmark dataset and evaluation protocol that includes simulated users with varying behaviors (e.g., noisy answers, inconsistent preferences). This allows for systematic evaluation and comparison of interactive retrieval systems.
selected.key_innovation: Provides a standardized way to evaluate interactive 3D retrieval systems, which is currently lacking, by simulating diverse user behaviors.
selected.addressed_gap: There is no standard benchmark for interactive 3D scene retrieval, making it hard to compare methods.

selected.idea_title: Domain-Adaptive Language-Visual Alignment for 3D Scene Retrieval
selected.idea_description: This idea focuses on adapting pre-trained 2D vision-language models (like CLIP) to 3D scenes by using domain adaptation techniques that account for the differences between 2D images and 3D data.
selected.key_innovation: Explicitly addresses the domain gap between 2D and 3D in language-visual alignment, which is often overlooked in existing transfer learning approaches.
selected.addressed_gap: Current methods transfer 2D models to 3D without domain adaptation, which may lead to suboptimal performance.

selected.idea_title: Personalized Interactive 3D Scene Retrieval via User Modeling
selected.idea_description: The system builds a model of the user based on past interactions and uses it to personalize the question generation and retrieval process. For example, it might ask more about colors if the user has shown preference for colorful scenes in the past.
selected.key_innovation: Personalizes the interactive refinement loop by learning user preferences and adapting the questioning strategy accordingly.
selected.addressed_gap: Existing systems treat all users the same, ignoring individual preferences and interaction histories.

selected.idea_title: Robust Interactive Retrieval with Noisy Answer Handling
selected.idea_description: We develop a method to handle noisy or incorrect answers from the user by estimating the reliability of answers and using multiple question strategies to cross-verify. The system can also ask clarifying questions when the answer is ambiguous.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces a noise-aware interactive retrieval system that can recover from incorrect or ambiguous user responses.
selected.addressed_gap: Current systems assume perfect user cooperation, but real users may provide noisy or incorrect answers.

selected.idea_title: Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Question Generation in 3D Scene Retrieval
selected.idea_description: Apply reinforcement learning to optimize the questioner's strategy over multiple interaction rounds, maximizing long-term retrieval accuracy based on cumulative user feedback. This approach enables the system to learn from past interactions and dynamically adjust questioning tactics.
selected.key_innovation: Shifts from static, single-turn EVPI-based question selection to a dynamic, learning-based adaptation that optimizes for long-term rewards in multi-turn dialogs.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors like EVPI focus on immediate information gain without considering long-term interaction efficiency or adaptation to user behavior, limiting optimization.

selected.idea_title: Efficient Training of Question-Answerer Agents via Knowledge Distillation
selected.idea_description: This research applies knowledge distillation to compress and accelerate the training of interactive retrieval models, making them more feasible for large-scale 3D datasets. A smaller student model learns from a larger teacher agent, retaining dialog effectiveness while reducing computational costs and latency in real-time applications.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces distillation techniques to optimize the question-answerer loop for efficiency, addressing scalability issues not tackled in predecessors that rely on computationally intensive training.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors face high computational demands; this addresses the gap in training efficiency, enabling deployment in resource-constrained settings or with massive scene databases.

selected.idea_title: Adaptive Stepsize Schedules for Gradient Bandits: Crossing the Regret Threshold
selected.idea_description: Investigate the use of adaptive stepsize schedules that start above the critical threshold and decrease over time. Determine if and how regret transitions from polynomial to logarithmic as the stepsize crosses the threshold, and design schedules that guarantee logarithmic regret without prior knowledge of the threshold.
selected.key_innovation: Combines constant-stepsize phase transition analysis with non-constant stepsize dynamics, providing a bridge between regimes and practical guidance for stepsize decay.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors focus exclusively on constant stepsizes; this addresses the practical scenario of decreasing stepsizes and how to design schedules that avoid polynomial regret.

selected.idea_title: Regret Phase Transitions in Softmax Policy Gradient Methods with Regularization
selected.idea_description: Extend the analysis of regret regimes to policy gradient methods that use softmax with entropy regularization or other regularizers. Examine how regularization shifts the critical threshold and alters the nature of the phase transition between logarithmic and polynomial regret.
selected.key_innovation: Generalizes the phase transition analysis to a broader class of algorithms, providing insights into how regularization affects the stability-regret trade-off.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors are specific to the standard gradient bandit algorithm without regularization; this explores the effect of a common modification on the regret threshold.

selected.idea_title: Sharp Thresholds for SGB under Sub-Gaussian and Heavy-Tailed Rewards
selected.idea_description: Analyze the threshold for the gradient bandit algorithm when rewards are sub-Gaussian or heavy-tailed, rather than bounded or Bernoulli. Determine how the threshold scales with reward distribution parameters, such as variance or tail index.
selected.key_innovation: Extends the threshold analysis to a broader class of reward distributions, which is more realistic for many applications and reveals distribution-dependent critical stepsizes.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors typically assume bounded or Bernoulli rewards; this addresses the gap in understanding how the threshold scales with reward distribution parameters.

selected.idea_title: Achieving Logarithmic Regret without Knowledge of the Gap
selected.idea_description: Design an algorithm that uses an adaptive stepsize based on observed reward differences to maintain a stepsize below the critical threshold without prior knowledge of the gap Δ. This could involve estimating Δ on the fly and adjusting the stepsize accordingly.
selected.key_innovation: Provides a practical algorithm that automatically adapts the stepsize to stay in the logarithmic regret regime, even when the gap is unknown, bridging theory and practice.
selected.addressed_gap: The threshold in the predecessor work depends on Δ; this addresses the practical challenge of unknown Δ and enables algorithms that work for any gap.

selected.idea_title: Automatic Stepsize Selection for Multi-Armed Gradient Bandits
selected.idea_description: Given the result that the stepsize must scale as O(1/K) for large K, develop a method to automatically set the stepsize based on the number of arms K to avoid polynomial regret. This could be a simple rule or an adaptive scheme that uses K as input.
selected.key_innovation: Provides a practical recommendation for stepsize choice in multi-armed bandits that scales with K, bridging the theory and practice of multi-armed extensions.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor work shows the necessity of scaling with K but does not provide a practical algorithm for choosing the stepsize in multi-armed settings.

selected.idea_title: High-Probability Regret Bounds for Stochastic Gradient Bandits
selected.idea_description: Derive high-probability regret bounds for the gradient bandit algorithm with constant stepsize, characterizing the threshold in a probabilistic sense. This would complement the expected regret analysis and provide risk-sensitive guarantees.
selected.key_innovation: Moves from expected regret to high-probability regret, which is more relevant for risk-averse applications and provides a finer-grained understanding of the phase transition.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors focus on expected regret; this addresses the gap in high-probability bounds and risk-sensitive performance guarantees.

selected.idea_title: Non-Stationary Stepsize Schedules: From Polynomial to Logarithmic Regret
selected.idea_description: Analyze the regret of SGB with a stepsize that changes over time in a non-stationary manner, e.g., initially large and then small, or varying with the estimated gap. The goal is to understand regret in such non-stationary settings and design schedules that minimize regret.
selected.key_innovation: Extends the analysis to non-constant stepsizes, which are more common in practice, and provides insights into how time-varying stepsizes affect regret regimes.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors assume constant stepsize; this addresses the gap in understanding time-varying stepsizes and their impact on the regret phase transition.

selected.idea_title: Multi-Armed Extensions: Scaling of Threshold with Number of Arms
selected.idea_description: For K>2, derive the exact threshold for the stepsize that separates logarithmic and polynomial regret, and how it scales with K. Examine the role of the gap structure (e.g., one large gap vs. multiple small gaps) in determining the threshold.
selected.key_innovation: Provides a detailed analysis of the threshold in multi-armed bandits, going beyond the two-armed case and characterizing how the threshold depends on the entire gap structure.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor work focuses on two arms or provides scaling with K; this addresses the gap in exact threshold characterization for multi-armed bandits with varying gap structures.

selected.idea_title: Adaptive Learning Rate Schedule Based on Regret Thresholds
selected.idea_description: Design an adaptive mechanism for the learning rate in stochastic gradient bandits (SGB) that adjusts dynamically using estimated suboptimality gaps to maintain logarithmic regret. This schedule would leverage the identified sharp threshold to scale the learning rate inversely with gap estimates, avoiding polynomial regret without prior knowledge.
selected.key_innovation: Uses the theoretical threshold as a real-time guide for adapting the learning rate, transforming a static analysis into a practical, data-driven policy.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the limitation of constant stepsizes requiring known gap parameters, enabling SGB to perform well in settings where gaps are unknown or vary.

selected.idea_title: Function-Approximated SGB with Regime Transitions
selected.idea_description: Extend SGB to settings with function approximation (e.g., linear or neural networks) and investigate if similar sharp thresholds exist for stepsize in terms of approximation error. Study how the regret regimes scale with model complexity.
selected.key_innovation: Generalizes tabular SGB to continuous state spaces, exploring the interplay between approximation, stepsize, and regret phase transitions.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the limitation to finite-armed settings, paving the way for applying SGB insights to more complex reinforcement learning tasks.

selected.idea_title: Nonlinear Extensions of Two-Stage Transformer Training Dynamics
selected.idea_description: Extend the linearized NTK framework to incorporate nonlinear activations in Transformers, analyzing how nonlinearities affect condensation and rank collapse stages. This involves deriving modified dynamics beyond the lazy regime to capture genuine feature learning.
selected.key_innovation: Integrating nonlinear effects into the previously linearized analysis, providing a more realistic model of Transformer training that accounts for activation functions.
selected.addressed_gap: The limitation of the NTK-based analysis in capturing genuine feature learning beyond the lazy regime due to nonlinearities in real Transformers.

selected.idea_title: Generalization to Alternative Attention Mechanisms
selected.idea_description: Apply the two-stage theory to variants like linear attention or cross-attention, examining if similar dynamics emerge and how architectural changes impact condensation and rank collapse. This includes theoretical and empirical comparisons.
selected.key_innovation: Extending the theoretical framework beyond standard self-attention to other attention forms, revealing universal or mechanism-specific behaviors in training dynamics.
selected.addressed_gap: The gap in understanding how different attention designs influence training dynamics and parameter evolution, as predecessors focus only on standard attention.

selected.idea_title: Depth-Dependent Analysis of Lazy Regime Escape
selected.idea_description: Investigate how the escape from the lazy regime via asymmetric perturbations scales with Transformer depth, identifying critical depths where dynamics shift and feature learning accelerates. Use both theory and experiments.
selected.key_innovation: Analyzing the interaction between depth and asymmetric perturbations to understand deep Transformer training stability and phase transitions.
selected.addressed_gap: The limitation of current analysis being possibly restricted to shallow models, with unclear effects in deeper networks where depth influences gradient flow and learning.

selected.idea_title: Hyperparameter Sensitivity of Condensation-Rank Collapse Theory
selected.idea_description: Empirically and theoretically study how learning rate, initialization scale, and batch size affect the predicted two-stage dynamics in Transformers, mapping hyperparameter regimes to distinct training behaviors.
selected.key_innovation: Connecting the theoretical dynamics to practical training hyperparameters, offering guidelines for optimal training regimes that leverage condensation and rank collapse.
selected.addressed_gap: The gap between idealized gradient flow analysis and real-world training with discrete steps and hyperparameters, as predecessors assume continuous dynamics.

selected.idea_title: Validation of Two-Stage Dynamics in Vision Transformers
selected.idea_description: Test the condensation and rank collapse theory on Vision Transformers (ViTs), comparing dynamics with NLP Transformers to assess universality across modalities and tasks.
selected.key_innovation: Applying the theory to a different modality (vision) to check if the same principles govern training, potentially validating or refining the theory for broader applications.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor papers focus on NLP; extending to ViTs addresses whether the theory is domain-general, filling a gap in cross-domain validation.

selected.idea_title: Theory-Driven Pruning of Redundant Attention Heads
selected.idea_description: Develop a pruning algorithm that predicts head redundancy based on condensation and rank collapse timing, enabling efficient model compression by removing heads during or after training.
selected.key_innovation: Using the theoretical understanding of dynamics to inform pruning schedules, potentially improving efficiency and performance over heuristic pruning methods.
selected.addressed_gap: The empirical observation of head redundancy lacks a principled pruning strategy based on training dynamics, as predecessors identify the gap but don't exploit it for efficiency.

selected.idea_title: Linking Parameter Dynamics to Attention Pattern Interpretability
selected.idea_description: Analyze how condensation and rank collapse in parameters correlate with changes in attention maps, enhancing interpretability by connecting theoretical dynamics to practical attention behaviors.
selected.key_innovation: Bridging the gap between theoretical parameter dynamics and empirical attention pattern analysis, providing a mechanistic explanation for interpretability observations.
selected.addressed_gap: The disconnect between theoretical accounts of parameters and practical observations of attention patterns, as predecessors focus on parameters but not directly on interpretability links.

selected.idea_title: Characterizing the Terminal Phase of Transformer Training
selected.idea_description: Define and study the terminal phase in Transformers akin to neural collapse in classifiers, examining if parameters converge to a low-rank structure with specific alignment properties after condensation.
selected.key_innovation: Extending the neural collapse concept to Transformer attention layers, identifying a stable endpoint of training that simplifies model structure.
selected.addressed_gap: The lack of a terminal-phase analysis for Transformers, as neural collapse in classifiers motivates but doesn't directly address attention parameter collapse.

selected.idea_title: Two-Stage Dynamics in Vision Transformers with Spatial Attention Patterns
selected.idea_description: Extend the condensation-rank collapse framework to Vision Transformers, analyzing how spatial attention heads condense around salient image regions before collapsing. Investigate if the dynamics differ fundamentally from sequence-based attention due to spatial locality and patch redundancy.
selected.key_innovation: Adapts the two-stage theory from sequential to spatial modality, revealing how image structure interacts with attention parameter collapse in a fundamentally different architecture.
selected.addressed_gap: Prior theory is validated only on sequence transformers; this explores whether the dynamics are universal or architecture/data-dependent, addressing a gap in generalizability.

selected.idea_title: Loss landscape geometry during condensation and rank collapse
selected.idea_description: Characterize how the loss landscape evolves through the two stages, using tools from optimization theory (e.g., Hessian spectrum). Test whether condensation corresponds to movement toward a flat basin and collapse corresponds to further simplification within it.
selected.key_innovation: Connects the parametric dynamics to the loss landscape, offering an optimization perspective on why the two stages occur.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors focus on parameter trajectories; this gap addresses how those trajectories relate to the structure of the loss surface.

selected.idea_title: Adaptive GP-UCB with Automatic Parameter Tuning
selected.idea_description: Develop a variant of GP-UCB that does not require prior knowledge of kernel smoothness or noise variance, yet achieves near-optimal Bayesian regret rates. The algorithm would dynamically adjust its exploration parameter based on observed data.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces a self-tuning mechanism for GP-UCB that removes the need for pre-specified parameters, making it more practical while preserving near-optimal regret.
selected.addressed_gap: Classical GP-UCB requires setting β_t based on unknown kernel and noise parameters, limiting its applicability in real-world scenarios.

selected.idea_title: Near-Optimal Regret for GP-UCB with Misspecified Priors
selected.idea_description: Analyze the regret of GP-UCB when the true function is not a sample from the assumed GP prior but lies in a related function class, such as an RKHS with a different kernel. Provide bounds that degrade gracefully with misspecification.
selected.key_innovation: Extends the refined information gain analysis to settings with model misspecification, providing robustness guarantees absent in prior work.
selected.addressed_gap: All predecessor analyses assume the true function is exactly a GP sample, which is unrealistic in practice.

selected.idea_title: Batched GP-UCB with Near-Optimal Regret Scaling
selected.idea_description: Design a batched version of GP-UCB that selects multiple points per round and prove that the cumulative regret scales near-optimally in the number of batches. Analyze how batch size affects regret and information gain.
selected.key_innovation: Applies the refined information gain control to the batched setting, demonstrating that parallel evaluations can be incorporated without sacrificing asymptotic rates.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors focus on sequential selection; practical applications often require parallel evaluations, but no near-optimal batch analysis exists for GP-UCB.

selected.idea_title: GP-UCB in Non-Stationary Environments with Drift
selected.idea_description: Extend the near-optimal regret analysis to non-stationary settings where the underlying function changes over time, using a time-varying GP model (e.g., via a drifting kernel). Derive regret bounds that adapt to the rate of change.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces a framework for handling non-stationarity in GP bandits while maintaining near-optimal regret through adaptive information gain control.
selected.addressed_gap: All predecessor analyses assume stationarity; real-world functions often change, but no near-optimal bounds exist for non-stationary GP bandits.

selected.idea_title: Sparse GP-UCB for Scalable Near-Optimal Regret
selected.idea_description: Use sparse GP approximations (e.g., inducing points) to reduce computational complexity of GP-UCB and analyze the resulting regret. Show that with a fixed number of inducing points, near-optimal regret can be maintained up to an additive term.
selected.key_innovation: Bridges the gap between computational efficiency and theoretical optimality in GP bandits by incorporating sparse approximations into the refined information gain analysis.
selected.addressed_gap: Standard GP-UCB scales cubically with time, making it impractical; sparse variants lack rigorous near-optimal regret guarantees.

selected.idea_title: Kernel Learning for GP-UCB with Joint Optimization
selected.idea_description: Develop a method that jointly learns kernel hyperparameters (e.g., length scales) and optimizes the function via GP-UCB, with a regret analysis that shows near-optimality. The algorithm would update the kernel based on observed data.
selected.key_innovation: Integrates kernel learning into the GP-UCB framework with theoretical guarantees, whereas predecessors assume a known kernel.
selected.addressed_gap: All predecessor analyses require a known kernel; in practice, kernel parameters are often unknown and must be estimated, but no near-optimal analysis exists for this setting.

selected.idea_title: High-Dimensional GP-UCB via Random Projections
selected.idea_description: Assume the objective function has low effective dimension and apply GP-UCB after random linear projections to a lower-dimensional space. Analyze regret in terms of the intrinsic dimension rather than the ambient dimension.
selected.key_innovation: Combines the refined information gain analysis with dimensionality reduction, enabling near-optimal regret in high-dimensional settings where direct GP-UCB fails.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors' regret bounds depend polynomially on the input dimension, making them prohibitive in high dimensions; no near-optimal reduction exists for GP bandits.

selected.idea_title: Constrained GP Bandits with Near-Optimal Regret
selected.idea_description: Optimize a function subject to unknown constraints (e.g., safety constraints) modeled by another GP, and design an algorithm that balances exploration and constraint satisfaction. Prove near-optimal regret bounds for the constrained setting.
selected.key_innovation: Extends the near-optimal regret analysis to constrained optimization, a common real-world requirement absent from predecessor works.
selected.addressed_gap: All predecessors address unconstrained optimization; constrained GP bandits lack near-optimal regret bounds.

selected.idea_title: Self-Normalized Martingale Concentration for Kernelized Information Gain Control
selected.idea_description: This work develops a general self-normalized martingale framework that unifies and refines information gain control across GP bandits and linear bandits. It aims to show that the refined concentration along the played sequence, as in GP-UCB, can be systematically captured and extended to other kernelized bandit algorithms.
selected.key_innovation: A unified technical framework that abstracts the information gain refinement, making it applicable beyond GP-UCB to a broader class of algorithms and settings.
selected.addressed_gap: The existing analysis is algorithm-specific; a unified framework would streamline and extend the refined information gain control to other settings and algorithms.

selected.idea_title: Information-Theoretic Regret for GP Bandits in High Dimensions
selected.idea_description: Improve the dimension dependence of the regret bounds for GP-UCB under Matérn kernels by exploiting low-dimensional structure (e.g., sparsity, additive models). Show that when the function has such structure, the information gain along the trajectory grows slower than the worst-case γ_T, leading to regret bounds that are nearly dimension-free.
selected.key_innovation: Leverages structural assumptions to achieve regret bounds that scale with the intrinsic rather than the ambient dimension, a significant improvement over kernel-dependent bounds.
selected.addressed_gap: Existing bounds have poor dependence on dimension, limiting applicability in high-dimensional problems; this addresses the challenge of scaling GP bandits to high dimensions with structural priors.

selected.idea_title: Using Cue-Suppression as a Regularizer for Robust Feature Learning
selected.idea_description: Incorporate the domain-agnostic suppression operations (e.g., texture, shape) as a form of data augmentation during training to encourage models to rely on a balanced set of visual cues. This would involve randomly suppressing different cues in training images and training the model to maintain performance, potentially improving robustness to real-world corruptions.
selected.key_innovation: Leveraging the suppression framework not just for evaluation but as an active training signal to actively shape and balance feature reliance in models.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor work uses suppression only for analysis; this addresses the opportunity to use the framework to proactively improve model robustness and reduce over-reliance on specific cues.

selected.idea_title: Extending Cue-Suppression to Video Understanding Tasks
selected.idea_description: Adapt the domain-agnostic suppression framework to video data by suppressing spatial cues (shape, texture) and temporal cues (motion) to evaluate feature reliance in video recognition models. This could reveal whether video models over-rely on local appearance versus temporal dynamics for tasks like action recognition.
selected.key_innovation: Applying the suppression idea to spatiotemporal data, defining novel suppression operations for video-specific cues and assessing architectural inductive biases in video models.
selected.addressed_gap: The current work is limited to static images; video understanding is a major domain with potential for different bias patterns that have not been systematically studied.

selected.idea_title: Analysis of Self-Supervised Vision Models via Cue-Suppression
selected.idea_description: Use the suppression framework to analyze feature reliance in self-supervised learning (SSL) models (e.g., SimCLR, MAE) and compare their shape/texture/color reliance to supervised models. Investigate whether SSL encourages different inductive biases, such as a stronger shape bias due to contrastive objectives.
selected.key_innovation: Applying the framework to SSL models, which learn without labels, to understand how pre-training objectives shape cue reliance.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor work focuses on supervised models; SSL is increasingly prevalent and its feature reliance patterns are not well understood or compared to supervised training.

selected.idea_title: Cue-Suppression for Detecting and Mitigating Spurious Correlations in Medical Imaging
selected.idea_description: Use the suppression framework to identify spurious cue reliance (e.g., on imaging artifacts or scanner-specific textures) in medical image classifiers, then apply suppression as a regularizer during training to encourage clinically relevant features. This could improve generalization across different hospitals or devices.
selected.key_innovation: Domain-specific application to medical imaging with a focus on detecting and mitigating spurious correlations that harm real-world deployment.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor work mentions medical imaging but does not delve into specific spurious correlations or propose active mitigation strategies; this addresses that gap.

selected.idea_title: Cross-Modal Cue-Suppression for Vision-Language Models
selected.idea_description: Extend the suppression framework to vision-language models (e.g., CLIP) by suppressing visual cues (shape, texture, color) and measuring the effect on cross-modal alignment (e.g., zero-shot classification or retrieval). This reveals whether these models rely on similar visual cues as pure vision models or develop different biases due to language supervision.
selected.key_innovation: Applying suppression to multi-modal models to understand how visual cues are processed in a cross-modal context, linking visual and linguistic representations.
selected.addressed_gap: The current work is limited to vision-only models; vision-language models are widespread and their visual cue reliance is unexplored with such controlled methods.

selected.idea_title: Robustness of the Suppression Framework to Different Suppression Methods
selected.idea_description: Conduct a systematic study of how sensitive the feature reliance measurements are to the choice of suppression method (e.g., different style transfer algorithms, GAN-based inpainting, or frequency filtering). Evaluate consistency across methods and propose best practices for reliable cue suppression.
selected.key_innovation: A methodological investigation into the suppression framework itself, assessing its robustness and developing more principled suppression operations.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor work introduces a specific suppression method but does not validate its robustness; this addresses potential methodological limitations and enhances the framework's reliability.

selected.idea_title: Architecture Search for Balanced Feature Reliance
selected.idea_description: Integrate the suppression framework as a performance metric in neural architecture search (NAS) to discover architectures with balanced reliance on shape, texture, and color. The search objective could include both accuracy and a measure of feature reliance (e.g., minimizing variance in performance across different suppressions).
selected.key_innovation: Using the suppression framework to guide automatic architecture design, actively seeking models with desired bias properties rather than just analyzing existing ones.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor work analyzes existing architectures; this actively explores the design space to find architectures with improved, balanced feature reliance.

selected.idea_title: Cue-Suppression for 3D Object Recognition from Point Clouds
selected.idea_description: Adapt the suppression framework to 3D data by suppressing shape (e.g., local geometry), texture (surface properties), or color in point clouds. Evaluate 3D recognition models (e.g., PointNet, DGCNN) to assess their reliance on local versus global shape and compare to 2D model biases.
selected.key_innovation: Extending the framework to 3D vision with appropriate suppression operations, enabling the study of feature reliance in a different modality.
selected.addressed_gap: The current work is 2D; 3D vision is a critical area with potentially different biases, and no systematic cue-suppression analysis exists for it.

selected.idea_title: Temporal cue suppression in video models: shape, texture, and motion reliance
selected.idea_description: Extend the domain-agnostic suppression framework to video recognition by developing controlled perturbations that selectively suppress spatial (shape, texture, color) and temporal (motion, temporal consistency) cues. Evaluate how architectures like 3D CNNs, Video Transformers, and hybrid models balance these cues across tasks such as action recognition and video object segmentation.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces a temporal dimension to cue suppression, enabling the first systematic study of motion-spatial cue interactions in video understanding.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor framework is limited to static images; this extends the cue-reliance analysis to dynamic visual data, addressing how models exploit spatiotemporal patterns.

selected.idea_title: Real-Time Cue Suppression for Adversarial Attack Mitigation
selected.idea_description: Implement cue suppression during inference to dynamically attenuate vulnerable cues in response to adversarial perturbations. This provides a defense mechanism by reducing reliance on easily manipulated cues in real-time applications.
selected.key_innovation: Applying cue suppression in real-time for adversarial robustness, addressing cue-specific vulnerabilities exploited by attacks.
selected.addressed_gap: Adversarial defenses often overlook cue-level weaknesses that attacks exploit, as revealed by spurious cue reliance studies.

selected.idea_title: Adaptive Orthogonality Loss for Dynamic Expert Specialization
selected.idea_description: Propose an orthogonality loss that dynamically adjusts its strength based on training progress or data distribution shifts, allowing experts to specialize more effectively over time. This could prevent premature specialization and encourage diversity as the model encounters varied tasks.
selected.key_innovation: Dynamic, context-aware adjustment of orthogonality constraints during training, rather than static application, to better adapt to evolving data patterns.
selected.addressed_gap: Static orthogonality losses in predecessors may not adapt to changing data or training phases, potentially limiting long-term specialization and robustness.

selected.idea_title: Variance-Inducing Routing with Curriculum Learning Strategies
selected.idea_description: Integrate the variance loss with curriculum learning techniques to gradually increase the discriminativeness of routing, starting with easier data or tasks. This could enhance training stability and lead to more effective specialization without destabilizing the load-balancing objectives.
selected.key_innovation: Scheduling the variance loss in a curriculum-based manner to smooth the training process and improve convergence to specialized expert assignments.
selected.addressed_gap: Abrupt application of variance loss in predecessors might disrupt training stability; curriculum learning addresses this by phasing in the loss gradually.

selected.idea_title: Orthogonality and Variance Losses for Multi-Modal Mixture-of-Experts Models
selected.idea_description: Extend orthogonality and variance losses to MoE architectures handling multiple modalities, such as text and images, to promote modality-specific or cross-modal expert specialization. This could improve performance on multi-task benchmarks by encouraging experts to focus on distinct input types or tasks.
selected.key_innovation: Applying the specialization-enhancing losses to multi-modal settings, where experts can specialize by modality, task, or combinations, beyond single-domain text models.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors focus on uni-modal (e.g., text or vision) MoE models; this addresses the gap in multi-modal specialization where expert overlap is likely high.

selected.idea_title: Post-Training Specialization via Orthogonality Regularization in Fine-Tuning
selected.idea_description: Use orthogonality loss as a regularization term during post-training or fine-tuning phases to refine expert specialization without retraining from scratch. This could enhance performance on downstream tasks by leveraging the load-balanced base model while adding discriminative routing.
selected.key_innovation: Introducing orthogonality loss specifically in post-training stages to improve specialization after initial balanced training, targeting performance gains on specific tasks.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors note that uniform routing degrades post-training performance; this directly addresses specialization improvement during fine-tuning phases.

selected.idea_title: Combining Orthogonality Loss with Knowledge Distillation for Efficient MoE
selected.idea_description: Integrate orthogonality loss with knowledge distillation from a dense teacher model to guide expert specialization in a compact student MoE model. This synergy could enhance efficiency and performance by aligning diverse expert outputs with teacher knowledge while promoting distinct expert roles.
selected.key_innovation: Joint optimization of orthogonality-induced diversity and distillation-based supervision to create efficient, specialized MoE models with minimal performance loss.
selected.addressed_gap: Balancing specialization and efficiency in resource-constrained settings; predecessors lack integration with distillation for better compact models.

selected.idea_title: Theoretical Analysis of Orthogonality and Variance Losses in MoE Optimization
selected.idea_description: Provide formal theoretical foundations for why orthogonality and variance losses improve expert specialization, using tools from optimization theory or information geometry. This could derive bounds on expert diversity and routing discriminativeness, explaining empirical observations from predecessors.
selected.key_innovation: Rigorous theoretical framework linking the losses to measurable properties like expert overlap and routing entropy, advancing beyond empirical validation.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors rely on empirical results; a theoretical understanding of the mechanisms behind orthogonality and variance losses is lacking.

selected.idea_title: Orthogonality-Aware Routing for BASE Layers and Alternative Sparse Frameworks
selected.idea_description: Adapt orthogonality and variance losses to BASE Layers and other sparse conditional computation methods, such as hashed routing or expert choice, to explicitly promote expert diversity. This could demonstrate the generality of the losses across different sparse architectures.
selected.key_innovation: Generalizing the losses to non-standard MoE frameworks beyond top-k routing, showing they are versatile tools for enhancing specialization in various sparse models.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors mention BASE Layers as compatible but do not explore integration; this addresses the gap in applying the losses to alternative sparse frameworks.

selected.idea_title: Dynamic Expert Pruning Based on Orthogonality Metrics for Efficiency
selected.idea_description: Use orthogonality scores computed during training to identify and prune redundant or overlapping experts dynamically, reducing model size and computational cost without sacrificing performance. This could lead to more efficient MoE models by eliminating under-specialized experts.
selected.key_innovation: Leveraging orthogonality as a pruning criterion for dynamic model compression, addressing redundancy directly from routing behavior.
selected.addressed_gap: Expert overlap and uniformity in predecessors can cause redundancy; pruning based on orthogonality improves efficiency and maintains specialization.

selected.idea_title: Optimal Transport Routing: Batch-Level Token-to-Expert Assignment for Guaranteed Diversity
selected.idea_description: Replace independent top-k gating with a mini-batch optimal transport formulation that jointly assigns tokens to experts by minimizing a cost combining expert capacity constraints with orthogonality and variance objectives. This produces globally balanced, maximally diverse assignments per batch rather than relying on auxiliary losses applied to independently sampled routes.
selected.key_innovation: Frames routing as a structured prediction / combinatorial optimization problem over the full batch, replacing post-hoc regularization with a principled transport objective that guarantees diversity by construction rather than by soft penalty.
selected.addressed_gap: Shazeer, GShard, and Switch Transformers all rely on independent per-token top-k decisions that auxiliary losses only softly regularize, leaving room for persistent expert overlap; optimal transport addresses this by making global diversity a hard constraint.

selected.idea_title: Variance-Induced Routing with Adaptive Entropy Regularization
selected.idea_description: Combine the variance-inducing loss with an adaptive entropy regularizer on the router's output distribution that is conditioned on the current batch's routing variance. As variance increases (more discriminative routing), the entropy penalty softly decreases, allowing sharper routing without collapse.
selected.key_innovation: Creates a feedback loop where increased routing discrimination (variance) automatically permits sharper expert choices (lower entropy), providing a self-regulating mechanism for stable specialization.
selected.addressed_gap: Address the instability that can arise when aggressively increasing routing variance, as the router may become overly sharp and lead to underutilization of experts without careful entropy tuning.

selected.idea_title: Power-law scaling of sparse attention emergence across diverse tasks
selected.idea_description: Investigate whether the power-law timing for sparse attention emergence holds across a variety of tasks beyond associative recall, and how the exponent depends on task complexity and structure. This would involve designing new benchmarks with varying degrees of pattern repetition and recall demands.
selected.key_innovation: Generalizes the power-law finding from a single controlled task to a broader set of tasks, potentially revealing universal scaling laws for sparse attention formation.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessor paper focused primarily on the associative recall task; this explores the generalizability of the power-law dynamics to more diverse and realistic tasks.

selected.idea_title: Impact of non-repetition data distribution properties on emergence
selected.idea_description: Systematically study how other data distribution properties—such as feature correlation, class imbalance, noise, and hierarchical structure—affect the timing and nature of sparse attention emergence. This would extend the analysis beyond simple repetition patterns.
selected.key_innovation: Expands the understanding of data-driven emergence from repetition to a comprehensive set of distributional factors, providing a more complete picture of data's role.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors emphasize repetition as a key driver; this explores the influence of other critical data distribution characteristics that are common in real-world scenarios.

selected.idea_title: Predictive models of optimizer and regularization effects on emergence timing
selected.idea_description: Conduct a systematic study varying optimizer choice (e.g., Adam, SGD, Lion) and regularization (e.g., weight decay, dropout) to quantitatively model their impact on the time-to-emergence. The goal is to derive a predictive formula or scaling law that incorporates these factors.
selected.key_innovation: Creates a quantitative mapping from optimizer and regularization hyperparameters to emergence timing, moving beyond qualitative observations to predictive modeling.
selected.addressed_gap: Grokking showed sensitivity to optimization and regularization, but a predictive relationship for sparse attention emergence timing is not established.

selected.idea_title: Testing sparse attention emergence in medium-scale transformers
selected.idea_description: Validate the theoretical predictions and toy-model findings in medium-scale transformer models (e.g., with millions of parameters) trained on more complex, but still controlled, datasets. This would assess the scalability of the power-law dynamics and the role of model capacity.
selected.key_innovation: Bridges the critical gap between highly simplified toy models and practical, larger-scale neural networks, testing the theory's real-world applicability.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors rely on toy models; this tests whether the emergent dynamics hold as model scale increases, which is essential for practical impact.

selected.idea_title: Co-evolution of sparse attention circuits with other model components
selected.idea_description: Analyze how the emergence of sparse attention heads interacts with the evolution of other components, such as the MLP layers, layer normalization, and residual connections. This would study the system-level dynamics to see if sparse attention emergence triggers or is triggered by changes elsewhere.
selected.key_innovation: Examines sparse attention emergence within the context of the full model architecture, rather than in isolation, revealing complex interdependencies.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors focus on attention heads in isolation; this addresses the lack of understanding about how attention circuits co-evolve with the rest of the network.

selected.idea_title: Architectural design principles to promote beneficial sparse attention
selected.idea_description: Use insights from emergence dynamics to design architectural modifications—such as specific initialization schemes, structural constraints, or inductive biases—that encourage the formation of useful sparse attention patterns earlier or more reliably during training.
selected.key_innovation: Shifts from observational analysis to prescriptive design, applying theoretical insights to engineer better architectures.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors are primarily observational; this addresses the opportunity to proactively design systems that leverage the emergent phenomena.

selected.idea_title: Unified theory of sparsity emergence across network representations
selected.idea_description: Develop a theoretical framework that connects the emergence of sparse attention with sparsity in other representations (e.g., feature sparsity in MLPs) under a common model of superposition, interference, and limited capacity. This would unify concepts from 'Toy Models of Superposition' with attention dynamics.
selected.key_innovation: Creates a unified model that explains sparsity across different parts of the network, linking attention head specialization to feature disentanglement in hidden layers.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors treat attention sparsity and representational superposition as separate lines of work; this integrates them into a single theoretical account.

selected.idea_title: Phase transition formalism unifying grokking and sparse attention emergence
selected.idea_description: Formally model both grokking and sparse attention emergence as phase transitions in the same dynamical system, identifying order parameters, critical exponents, and universality classes. This would provide a mathematical framework that connects these seemingly different phenomena.
selected.key_innovation: Offers a rigorous mathematical formalism that captures both phenomena as instances of a broader class of phase transitions in learning dynamics.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors treat grokking and sparse attention emergence as related but separate; this provides a unified theoretical model that explains both.

selected.idea_title: Multi-Head Superposition Dynamics in Sparse Attention Formation
selected.idea_description: Extend the Toy Models of Superposition framework to analyze how multiple attention heads compete for limited representational capacity during sparse attention emergence. Investigate whether repeated data patterns not only drive sparsity but also create feature competition that predicts which heads specialize for which associative recalls.
selected.key_innovation: Applying superposition theory not just to features but to the emergence and specialization of multiple attention heads within a single model layer.
selected.addressed_gap: The predecessors analyze single head induction mechanics or general feature superposition, but lack a theory for how multiple heads interact and partition tasks during sparse attention formation.

selected.idea_title: Emergence Dynamics as a Diagnostic Probe for Training Data Quality and Contamination
selected.idea_description: Propose that the timing, sharpness, and characteristics of sparse attention emergence serve as a sensitive diagnostic signal for properties of the training data that are difficult to measure directly. Test whether data contamination (e.g., benchmark memorization), data deduplication, or distribution shift systematically alter emergence signatures, enabling a novel 'emergence fingerprinting' method for data auditing.
selected.key_innovation: Repurposes the theoretical understanding of emergence dynamics from a research object into a practical diagnostic tool, inverting the usual direction of inquiry: instead of asking 'what does data do to emergence,' ask 'what does emergence tell us about data?'
selected.addressed_gap: While predecessors show emergence is sensitive to data properties, no one has explored whether emergence dynamics can be used as a measurement instrument for data characteristics, addressing the growing challenge of training data quality assessment.

selected.idea_title: Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive State-Action Graph Refinement
selected.idea_description: Integrate reinforcement learning to iteratively refine the State-Action Graph based on real-world interaction outcomes, allowing the system to improve predictions through trial and error. This approach uses rewards from embodied experiences to update graph edges and nodes dynamically.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces a feedback loop from interactions to adapt the graph beyond static LLM-VLM priors, enabling continuous learning in dynamic environments.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the limitation in SAGE where graph construction relies solely on initial LLM proposals and VLM checks, lacking adaptation based on real-world feedback.

selected.idea_title: Efficient VLM-Grounded Graph Construction with Active Query Selection
selected.idea_description: Develop an active learning strategy to select the most informative queries to VLMs during graph construction, reducing computational cost while maintaining accuracy. This involves prioritizing queries that resolve ambiguities or fill knowledge gaps in the state-action graph.
selected.key_innovation: Novel query optimization for efficient grounding in open-world scenarios, minimizing VLM calls without sacrificing graph quality.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses scalability and efficiency issues in current methods that require extensive VLM interactions, as seen in SAGE's reliance on multiple models.

selected.idea_title: Physics-Informed State Transition Modeling Using Simulation Engines
selected.idea_description: Integrate physics simulation engines to predict and validate state transitions for physical interactions, enhancing temporal reasoning accuracy. The system uses simulations to ground language-defined transitions in physical laws, refining the graph with simulated outcomes.
selected.key_innovation: Combines symbolic state graphs with continuous physics models for more realistic and physically plausible transitions.
selected.addressed_gap: Enhances the temporal causal reasoning from CLEVRER by incorporating physics priors, which are missing in pure language-grounded approaches like SAGE.

selected.idea_title: Multimodal State Recognition with Audio-Visual-Language Fusion
selected.idea_description: Extend state recognition to incorporate audio cues alongside visual and language inputs, enabling richer understanding of object states in dynamic environments. For example, using sound from interactions to infer state changes like breaking or sizzling.
selected.key_innovation: Fusion of audio modality with existing vision-language models for state grounding, leveraging auditory signals for additional context.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the limitation in predecessors like CLIP and SAGE that focus primarily on vision and language, underutilizing other sensory modalities.

selected.idea_title: Probabilistic State-Action Graphs for Uncertainty-Aware Reasoning
selected.idea_description: Model uncertainties in state assignments and transitions using probabilistic graphical models, allowing the system to make decisions under uncertainty. This involves assigning confidence scores to graph elements and using Bayesian inference for reasoning.
selected.key_innovation: Incorporates probabilistic inference into the graph structure for more reliable and nuanced reasoning in ambiguous scenarios.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the gap in handling noisy or ambiguous data in open-world scenarios, as predecessors like SAGE assume deterministic groundings.

selected.idea_title: Hierarchical Decomposition for Complex Action Sequences
selected.idea_description: Develop a hierarchical State-Action Graph that decomposes high-level actions into nested sub-states and sub-actions, enabling reasoning over complex, multi-step tasks. This uses LLMs to generate hierarchies and VLMs to ground them in videos.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces hierarchy in the graph to manage task complexity, allowing abstraction and composition across scales.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the limitation in handling long-horizon or intricate tasks, as predecessors like Visual Genome and Neural State Machine focus on flat or simple structures.

selected.idea_title: Zero-Shot Domain Transfer via Compositional Concept Alignment
selected.idea_description: Leverage the compositional nature of attributes and actions to transfer knowledge to new domains without fine-tuning, by aligning concepts across domains using language embeddings. For instance, transferring state concepts from cooking to industrial scenarios.
selected.key_innovation: Uses compositional generalization for cross-domain adaptation, building on Attributes-as-Operators to enable zero-shot transfer.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the challenge of generalizing to unseen domains with limited data, as predecessors like CLIP and SAGE may struggle with domain shifts.

selected.idea_title: Interactive Graph Refinement with Human-in-the-Loop Feedback
selected.idea_description: Allow human users to provide corrective feedback on the State-Action Graph during or after construction, improving accuracy and trust. This system integrates human annotations or corrections directly into the graph updating process.
selected.key_innovation: Integrates human feedback directly into the graph refinement process, combining human expertise with automated methods.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses potential errors or biases in LLM-VLM outputs, as seen in SayCan and Socratic Models, by incorporating human oversight for robustness.

selected.idea_title: Cross-Modal State Concept Transfer via Language Grounding
selected.idea_description: Extend SAGE's language-grounded State-Action Graph to incorporate non-visual modalities such as audio or tactile data, enabling state recognition across sensory inputs. This leverages CLIP's multimodal alignment to ground states in diverse data streams for richer environmental understanding.
selected.key_innovation: Transferring state concepts across modalities using language as a bridge, moving beyond visual-only state modeling.
selected.addressed_gap: Predecessors like SAGE focus primarily on visual states; this opens applications in multi-sensory environments and addresses modality limitations.

selected.idea_title: Embodied Graph Exploration for Autonomous Discovery
selected.idea_description: Deploy an embodied agent in a simulated environment to actively explore and update the State-Action Graph through interaction, using reward signals from VLM assessments of novel state transitions.
selected.key_innovation: Creates an active learning system where the graph expands through exploration rather than passive observation, inspired by SayCan's grounding but with discovery-focused objectives.
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the limitation of building graphs only from existing data, enabling discovery of novel state-action relationships.

selected.idea_title: Hierarchical Phase Diffusion for Granular Motion Synthesis
selected.idea_description: This idea proposes a multi-level diffusion model that operates over a hierarchy of phase variables—global action phases, local limb phases, and joint-specific phases—to generate motion with finer-grained compositional control. The model would use separate denoising processes for each level, guided by cross-attention between layers, enabling synthesis of complex, multi-part actions that are not well-captured by a single flat phase representation.
selected.key_innovation: Introduces a nested, hierarchical structure for phase-conditioned diffusion, allowing independent control and compositional guidance at different scales of motion detail (e.g., whole body vs. individual limbs).
selected.addressed_gap: Addresses the limitation of prior phase representations (e.g., in PFNN and ACT-PAE) that use a single, global phase variable, which struggles to represent independent, concurrent sub-actions or highly detailed limb coordination.

selected.idea_title: Cross-Modal Motion Diffusion with Joint Visual-Textual Phase Guidance
selected.idea_description: This research would develop a diffusion framework that jointly conditions on both text prompts and short video clips to guide motion generation. The key is to align the visual content from the video and the semantic content from text within a shared phase-aware latent space, using a two-stream encoder and a diffusion model that composes guidance from both modalities through a unified loss and sampling process.
selected.key_innovation: Fuses visual conditioning (from video) with textual conditioning in a phase-aware manner, going beyond either text-only or action-conditioned models by providing richer, more grounded guidance for synthesis.
selected.addressed_gap: Closes the gap in controllability and specificity left by text-only models like MDM, which can be ambiguous, and by video-conditioned models that may lack semantic compositionality for long-horizon sequences.

selected.idea_title: Diffusion for Asynchronous Multi-Character Interaction Synthesis
selected.idea_description: This idea extends compositional phase diffusion to generate plausible interactions between two or more characters, where each has its own phase-informed motion trajectory. The model would use a graph-based attention mechanism to condition each character's denoising process on the phase and semantic state of the others, enabling synchronized or reactive movements without pre-scripted interaction datasets.
